facts.text: Barry Bonds holds the all-time MLB home run record with 762 career home runs, surpassing Hank Aaron's record of 755 in 2007.
Pete Rose holds the all-time MLB hits record with 4,256 career hits, earning him the nickname 'Charlie Hustle.'
Nolan Ryan holds the all-time MLB strikeout record with 5,714 career strikeouts across 27 seasons.
Cy Young holds the all-time MLB wins record with 511 career victories, a record considered virtually unbreakable in the modern era.
Rickey Henderson holds the all-time stolen base record with 1,406 career steals, far ahead of second-place Lou Brock's 938.
Hank Aaron held the all-time home run record at 755 for 33 years, from 1974 until Barry Bonds broke it in 2007.
Babe Ruth's career record of 714 home runs stood for 39 years until Hank Aaron broke it on April 8, 1974.
Cal Ripken Jr. played in 2,632 consecutive games from 1982 to 1998, breaking Lou Gehrig's 'Iron Horse' record of 2,130.
Joe DiMaggio's 56-game hitting streak in 1941 is considered one of the most unbreakable records in all of sports.
Barry Bonds set the single-season home run record with 73 in 2001, breaking Mark McGwire's record of 70 set in 1998.
The 1927 New York Yankees, known as 'Murderers' Row,' are often considered the greatest baseball team ever assembled, going 110-44.
Jackie Robinson broke MLB's color barrier on April 15, 1947, playing first base for the Brooklyn Dodgers. His number 42 is retired across all of MLB.
Larry Doby became the first Black player in the American League on July 5, 1947, just 11 weeks after Jackie Robinson's debut.
The New York Yankees have won 27 World Series championships, more than any other MLB franchise.
The St. Louis Cardinals have the second-most World Series titles with 11 championships.
The Boston Red Sox broke the 'Curse of the Bambino' by winning the 2004 World Series, their first championship since 1918.
The Chicago Cubs broke the 'Curse of the Billy Goat' by winning the 2016 World Series, ending a 108-year championship drought.
The 'Curse of the Bambino' began when the Red Sox sold Babe Ruth to the Yankees in 1920 and didn't win another World Series until 2004.
The 'Shot Heard Round the World' was Bobby Thomson's walk-off homer for the New York Giants against the Brooklyn Dodgers in the 1951 NL playoff.
Kirk Gibson's pinch-hit walk-off home run in Game 1 of the 1988 World Series is one of the most iconic moments in baseball history. He could barely walk due to injuries.
Bill Buckner's error at first base in Game 6 of the 1986 World Series allowed the New York Mets to complete a miraculous comeback against the Red Sox.
The Pine Tar Game of 1983 involved George Brett's home run being initially disallowed because pine tar on his bat exceeded the 18-inch limit. The ruling was later overturned.
Don Larsen pitched the only perfect game in World Series history on October 8, 1956, for the New York Yankees against the Brooklyn Dodgers.
In 2004, the Red Sox became the first team in MLB history to come back from a 3-0 deficit in a postseason series, defeating the Yankees in the ALCS.
The 1919 'Black Sox' scandal involved eight Chicago White Sox players accused of intentionally losing the World Series against the Cincinnati Reds.
Shoeless Joe Jackson, banned for his alleged involvement in the 1919 Black Sox scandal, had a .356 career batting average — third highest in MLB history.
The designated hitter rule was adopted by the American League in 1973. The National League didn't adopt a universal DH until 2022.
The first World Series was played in 1903 between the Boston Americans (Red Sox) and the Pittsburgh Pirates. Boston won five games to three.
The 1994 World Series was never played due to the players' strike, the first cancellation since 1904.
Mariano Rivera is the all-time saves leader with 652 career saves and was the first player unanimously elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame.
Nolan Ryan threw a record seven no-hitters during his career, the last coming at age 44 in 1991.
In 2010, there were three perfect games thrown in a single season — by Dallas Braden, Roy Halladay, and controversially lost by Armando Galarraga on a blown call.
Sandy Koufax pitched a perfect game on September 9, 1965, against the Cubs. He threw four no-hitters in his career.
The Big Red Machine — the 1970s Cincinnati Reds led by Pete Rose, Johnny Bench, Joe Morgan, and Tony Perez — won back-to-back World Series in 1975 and 1976.
The Oakland A's won three consecutive World Series from 1972 to 1974, led by Reggie Jackson, Catfish Hunter, and Rollie Fingers.
The New York Yankees won five consecutive World Series from 1949 to 1953, a feat never matched before or since.
The Atlanta Braves won 14 consecutive division titles from 1991 to 2005 but only converted one into a World Series championship (1995).
The San Francisco Giants won three World Series in five years (2010, 2012, 2014), all in even-numbered years, earning the 'Even Year Magic' nickname.
The Yankees-Red Sox rivalry is considered the greatest in baseball, intensified by the Babe Ruth trade in 1920 and the 2003-2004 ALCS matchups.
The Cubs-Cardinals rivalry is one of baseball's oldest, with both teams based in the Midwest and competing in the NL Central.
The Dodgers-Giants rivalry dates back to when both teams were in New York City. It continued when they both moved to California in 1958.
Babe Ruth called his shot in Game 3 of the 1932 World Series, allegedly pointing to center field before hitting a home run, though the truth is debated.
Lou Gehrig's 'Luckiest Man' speech on July 4, 1939, at Yankee Stadium is one of the most famous moments in sports history. He was diagnosed with ALS.
Ted Williams is the last player to hit .400 in a season, batting .406 in 1941. He refused to sit out the final day to protect his average.
Babe Ruth hit 60 home runs in 1927, a single-season record that stood for 34 years until Roger Maris hit 61 in 1961.
Roger Maris hit 61 home runs in 1961, breaking Babe Ruth's record, but Commissioner Ford Frick controversially added an asterisk because Maris had a 162-game season versus Ruth's 154.
Willie Mays made 'The Catch' — an over-the-shoulder grab in deep center field during Game 1 of the 1954 World Series — considered the greatest defensive play in baseball history.
Mickey Mantle hit the longest measured home run in MLB history, estimated at 565 feet, at Griffith Stadium in Washington on April 17, 1953.
Roberto Clemente died in a plane crash on December 31, 1972, while delivering earthquake relief supplies to Nicaragua. He had exactly 3,000 career hits.
Ty Cobb held a .366 career batting average, the highest in MLB history, and was the first class inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in 1936.
Hank Aaron received death threats as he approached Babe Ruth's home run record in 1974, requiring him to travel with bodyguards and receive FBI protection.
Ken Griffey Jr.'s sweet swing and backwards cap made him one of the most popular players of the 1990s. He hit 630 career home runs.
Derek Jeter ended his career with a walk-off single in his final game at Yankee Stadium on September 25, 2014. He had 3,465 career hits.
Shohei Ohtani became the first player since Babe Ruth to be a full-time hitter and starting pitcher, earning AL MVP in 2021 and 2023.
In 2023, Shohei Ohtani signed a 10-year, $700 million contract with the Los Angeles Dodgers, the largest contract in professional sports history at the time.
Mike Trout won three AL MVP awards (2014, 2016, 2019) before turning 28, and is widely considered a generational talent despite playing for the often-struggling Angels.
Greg Maddux won four consecutive Cy Young Awards from 1992 to 1995, an unprecedented feat of pitching dominance.
Pedro Martinez's 1999 and 2000 seasons with the Red Sox are considered among the most dominant pitching performances in the modern era, with ERAs of 2.07 and 1.74.
Randy Johnson struck out 20 batters in a nine-inning game on May 8, 2001. His 6-foot-10 frame made him one of the most intimidating pitchers ever.
Randy Johnson once killed a bird with a pitch during a spring training game in 2001. The dove exploded on contact with his fastball.
Ichiro Suzuki set the single-season hits record with 262 hits in 2004, breaking George Sisler's 84-year-old record of 257.
Ichiro Suzuki had over 4,000 combined professional hits between his careers in Japan's NPB and MLB.
Albert Pujols hit his 700th career home run in 2022, becoming only the fourth player in MLB history to reach that milestone.
David Ortiz, known as 'Big Papi,' hit .688 with 2 home runs in the 2013 World Series, leading the Red Sox to the championship after the Boston Marathon bombing.
Clayton Kershaw threw a perfect game through 7 innings multiple times and won three Cy Young Awards (2011, 2013, 2014) with the Dodgers.
Aaron Judge set the American League single-season home run record with 62 in 2022, breaking Roger Maris's 61-year-old record.
Pete Rose was permanently banned from baseball in 1989 for gambling on games while managing the Cincinnati Reds, making him ineligible for the Hall of Fame ballot.
Mariano Rivera's cut fastball, or 'cutter,' is considered the most devastating single pitch in baseball history. He threw it almost exclusively.
Fenway Park in Boston, opened in 1912, is the oldest active MLB ballpark. Its iconic left field wall is known as the 'Green Monster.'
The Green Monster at Fenway Park stands 37 feet tall and is 310 feet from home plate. It has a manual scoreboard that is still updated by hand from inside the wall.
Wrigley Field in Chicago, home of the Cubs, is famous for its ivy-covered outfield walls, which were planted in 1937.
Wrigley Field didn't install permanent lights for night games until 1988, making it the last MLB park to host night games.
The original Yankee Stadium, known as 'The House That Ruth Built,' opened in 1923 and was replaced by a new Yankee Stadium across the street in 2009.
Dodger Stadium in Los Angeles, opened in 1962, has the largest seating capacity in MLB at approximately 56,000.
The Houston Astrodome, opened in 1965, was the first domed stadium and was dubbed the 'Eighth Wonder of the World.'
Camden Yards in Baltimore, opened in 1992, pioneered the retro-modern ballpark design that influenced nearly every stadium built after it.
The Brooklyn Dodgers moved to Los Angeles and the New York Giants moved to San Francisco after the 1957 season, devastating New York fans.
The Milwaukee Braves moved to Atlanta in 1966, and the Seattle Pilots became the Milwaukee Brewers after just one season in 1970.
The Montreal Expos became the Washington Nationals in 2005, ending MLB's presence in Canada outside of Toronto.
The Arizona Diamondbacks and Tampa Bay Devil Rays joined MLB as expansion teams in 1998, bringing the total to 30 teams.
The New York Mets and Houston Colt .45s (later Astros) were added as expansion teams in 1962 to fill the void left by departing NL teams in New York.
A regulation baseball has exactly 108 hand-stitched double stitches (216 individual stitches) made of red cotton thread.
Baseballs used in MLB games are manufactured by Rawlings in Costa Rica. Each ball has a cork and rubber core wrapped in yarn and covered in cowhide leather.
MLB goes through approximately 900,000 baseballs per season. Each ball is used for an average of only six pitches before being replaced.
MLB bats must be made of solid wood, typically ash, maple, or birch. Aluminum bats are used in college and amateur leagues but banned in professional play.
The maximum length of an MLB bat is 42 inches, and there is no weight restriction, though most players use bats weighing 31-34 ounces.
Baseball gloves weren't commonly used until the 1870s. Early catchers caught barehanded, leading to frequent hand injuries.
The modern baseball glove with a webbed pocket wasn't introduced until the 1920s. Before that, fielders used flat, padded gloves.
The first baseball cards were included in cigarette packs in the 1880s. The most valuable card ever sold is a 1909 T206 Honus Wagner, which fetched $7.25 million in 2022.
'Take Me Out to the Ball Game,' written in 1908 by Jack Norworth and Albert Von Tilzer, is the third-most performed song in America after the national anthem and 'Happy Birthday.'
Neither Jack Norworth nor Albert Von Tilzer had ever attended a baseball game when they wrote 'Take Me Out to the Ball Game.'
The Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York, was established in 1936. The first five inductees were Ty Cobb, Babe Ruth, Honus Wagner, Christy Mathewson, and Walter Johnson.
The Little League World Series has been held annually in Williamsport, Pennsylvania, since 1947, with teams from around the world competing.
Spring training takes place each February and March in Florida's 'Grapefruit League' and Arizona's 'Cactus League.'
The minor league system has four main levels: Rookie, Single-A, Double-A, and Triple-A, serving as a development pipeline for MLB talent.
Field of Dreams (1989) features the famous line 'If you build it, he will come.' MLB held a real game at the movie's Iowa cornfield location in 2021.
Bull Durham (1988) is considered one of the greatest sports movies ever made, starring Kevin Costner, Susan Sarandon, and Tim Robbins.
Major League (1989) featured the fictional Cleveland Indians and the catchphrase 'Just a bit outside' by announcer Harry Doyle, played by Bob Uecker.
Moneyball (2011), starring Brad Pitt, depicted the Oakland A's use of sabermetrics under GM Billy Beane, revolutionizing how teams evaluate players.
A League of Their Own (1992) told the story of the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League during World War II, featuring the iconic line 'There's no crying in baseball!'
The infield fly rule, one of baseball's most confusing rules, prevents infielders from intentionally dropping pop flies to turn double plays with runners on base.
The balk rule, where a pitcher makes an illegal motion on the mound, is considered one of the most subjective and debated rules in baseball.
MLB introduced a pitch clock in 2023, requiring pitchers to deliver within 15 seconds with bases empty and 20 seconds with runners on, dramatically speeding up games.
The 2023 pitch clock reduced average MLB game time by about 26 minutes, from 3 hours 3 minutes to 2 hours 37 minutes.
MLB banned defensive shifts starting in 2023, requiring teams to have two infielders on each side of second base when a pitch is delivered.
The save rule was introduced in 1969. A pitcher earns a save by finishing a game won by his team while meeting certain criteria regarding the lead and innings pitched.
The 1969 'Miracle Mets' went from being one of the worst teams in baseball to winning the World Series, defeating the heavily favored Baltimore Orioles.
The 2001 World Series between the Diamondbacks and Yankees is considered one of the greatest ever, with Arizona winning Game 7 on Luis Gonzalez's walk-off single off Mariano Rivera.
The 2011 World Series Game 6 between the Cardinals and Rangers featured the Cardinals twice being one strike away from elimination before David Freese's walk-off homer.
The 2016 World Series Game 7 between the Cubs and Indians went to extra innings, with the Cubs winning 8-7 in 10 innings to end their 108-year drought.
The 2017 Houston Astros were later found to have used an electronic sign-stealing scheme during their World Series-winning season, resulting in major suspensions and fines.
The 2020 World Series was played during the COVID-19 pandemic, with the Dodgers defeating the Rays in six games at a neutral site in Arlington, Texas.
The Cleveland Guardians changed their name from the Indians in 2022, after being called the Indians since 1915.
The 1998 home run race between Mark McGwire (70) and Sammy Sosa (66) captivated America, though both players were later linked to performance-enhancing drugs.
The 'Steroid Era' of the late 1990s and early 2000s cast a shadow over many records, leading to congressional hearings in 2005.
The Mitchell Report, released in 2007, named 89 MLB players alleged to have used performance-enhancing drugs, including Roger Clemens and Miguel Tejada.
Roger Clemens won seven Cy Young Awards, the most in MLB history, but has not been elected to the Hall of Fame due to PED allegations.
Walter Johnson holds the career shutout record with 110, a record that has stood since 1927 and is considered unbreakable.
Cy Young's 511 career wins are 94 more than second-place Walter Johnson's 417. No active pitcher is remotely close to either total.
Tom Seaver holds the record for the highest percentage of votes on a Hall of Fame ballot at 98.84%, until Mariano Rivera received 100% in 2019.
Craig Kimbrel and Kenley Jansen both surpassed 400 career saves, joining an exclusive club that includes Mariano Rivera, Trevor Hoffman, and Lee Smith.
Trevor Hoffman was the first pitcher to record 600 career saves before Mariano Rivera passed him with 652.
The 1995 World Series was the Atlanta Braves' only championship during their 14-year division title run, defeating the Cleveland Indians in six games.
The Florida Marlins (now Miami Marlins) have won two World Series (1997, 2003) and were dismantled through trades after each championship.
The Kansas City Royals won the 2015 World Series, their first championship since 1985, defeating the New York Mets in five games.
The Washington Nationals won their first World Series in 2019, coming back from a 19-31 start to the season to ultimately defeat the Houston Astros.
In the 2019 World Series, the road team won every single game — the first time that had ever happened in a seven-game World Series.
The Texas Rangers won their first World Series in franchise history in 2023, defeating the Arizona Diamondbacks, also winning every game on the road.
Casey Stengel managed the Yankees to seven World Series titles in 12 years (1949-1960 as manager), making him one of the greatest managers in history.
Joe Torre managed the Yankees to four World Series titles in five years (1996, 1998, 1999, 2000), establishing a modern dynasty.
The 1998 Yankees went 114-48 in the regular season and 11-2 in the postseason, often cited as the best single-season team in MLB history.
Tommy Lasorda managed the Dodgers for 21 seasons and famously said he bled 'Dodger blue.' He won two World Series titles (1981, 1988).
Connie Mack managed the Philadelphia Athletics for 50 years (1901-1950), the longest managerial tenure in MLB history, accumulating both the most wins and most losses.
Tony La Russa is third on the all-time managerial wins list with 2,728 victories across stints with the White Sox, A's, and Cardinals.
The 1960 World Series ended with Bill Mazeroski's walk-off home run in Game 7 for the Pittsburgh Pirates — the first walk-off homer to end a World Series.
Joe Carter's walk-off home run in Game 6 of the 1993 World Series won the championship for the Toronto Blue Jays, only the second time a Series ended on a homer.
The Toronto Blue Jays are the only non-U.S. team to win the World Series, winning back-to-back in 1992 and 1993.
The 1975 World Series Game 6 between the Red Sox and Reds is often called the greatest World Series game ever, ending on Carlton Fisk's dramatic walk-off homer.
Carlton Fisk's home run in the 1975 World Series is famous for the TV footage of him waving the ball fair as it flew down the left field line at Fenway Park.
Reggie Jackson earned the nickname 'Mr. October' by hitting three home runs on three consecutive pitches in Game 6 of the 1977 World Series.
The 2003 ALCS featured the Aaron Boone walk-off homer in Game 7 for the Yankees, breaking Red Sox hearts before Boston's historic 2004 comeback.
In Game 4 of the 2004 ALCS, Dave Roberts' stolen base off Mariano Rivera sparked the Red Sox comeback from a 3-0 series deficit.
Curt Schilling pitched Game 6 of the 2004 ALCS with a surgically sutured ankle, famously showing the 'bloody sock' that became a symbol of the Red Sox comeback.
The 'Subway Series' of 2000 between the Yankees and Mets was the first New York cross-town World Series since 1956.
The 2005 World Series saw the Chicago White Sox sweep the Houston Astros for their first championship since 1917, ending an 88-year drought.
Mark Buehrle threw a perfect game on July 23, 2009, for the White Sox. He also threw a no-hitter in 2007.
Roy Halladay threw a no-hitter in the 2010 NLDS against the Reds — only the second postseason no-hitter in MLB history after Don Larsen's perfect game.
Roy Halladay also threw a perfect game during the 2010 regular season, making him one of only a few pitchers to throw both a perfect game and a postseason no-hitter.
Felix Hernandez threw a perfect game on August 15, 2012, for the Seattle Mariners despite pitching for a team that finished below .500.
Philip Humber of the White Sox threw a perfect game on April 21, 2012 — one of the most unlikely perfect games, given his journeyman career.
There have been only 24 perfect games in MLB history, making it one of the rarest achievements in professional sports.
Johnny Vander Meer is the only pitcher in MLB history to throw consecutive no-hitters, accomplishing the feat on June 11 and June 15, 1938.
Dock Ellis allegedly threw a no-hitter on June 12, 1970, while under the influence of LSD, one of baseball's most bizarre stories.
The longest game in MLB history lasted 26 innings — a 1-1 tie between the Brooklyn Robins and Boston Braves on May 1, 1920.
The longest game by time in MLB history was a 25-inning contest between the White Sox and Brewers in 1984, lasting 8 hours and 6 minutes over two days.
Fernando Valenzuela's debut with the Dodgers in 1981 sparked 'Fernandomania,' as the Mexican-born left-hander won his first eight starts and became a cultural phenomenon.
Hideo Nomo's arrival with the Dodgers in 1995 broke the barrier for Japanese players in MLB and renewed interest in baseball across the Pacific.
The posting system allows Japanese players to negotiate with MLB teams. It brought stars like Ichiro, Daisuke Matsuzaka, Masahiro Tanaka, and Shohei Ohtani to America.
The 1994 strike canceled the World Series and nearly 1,000 regular-season games, severely damaging baseball's popularity with fans.
Cal Ripken Jr.'s 2,131st consecutive game on September 6, 1995, breaking Lou Gehrig's record, is credited with helping restore fan interest after the 1994 strike.
The All-Star Game has been played annually since 1933, except for 1945 (wartime travel restrictions) and 2020 (COVID-19 pandemic).
From 2003 to 2016, the All-Star Game determined which league had home-field advantage in the World Series, a controversial arrangement.
Ted Williams hit a walk-off three-run homer to win the 1941 All-Star Game at Briggs Stadium in Detroit.
The American League won 13 consecutive All-Star Games from 1997 to 2009, the longest winning streak in All-Star history.
Satchel Paige became the oldest player to debut in MLB at age 42 in 1948 with the Cleveland Indians, after a legendary career in the Negro Leagues.
Satchel Paige made his last MLB appearance in 1965 at age 59, pitching three scoreless innings for the Kansas City Athletics.
Josh Gibson, a legendary Negro Leagues catcher, is estimated to have hit nearly 800 home runs in his career. He was inducted into the Hall of Fame in 1972.
In 2024, MLB officially incorporated Negro Leagues statistics into its record books, recognizing the achievements of players like Josh Gibson, whose .372 average tops Ty Cobb's .366.
The Negro Leagues produced many of baseball's greatest players, including Jackie Robinson, Satchel Paige, Josh Gibson, Cool Papa Bell, and Buck O'Neil.
Cool Papa Bell was said to be so fast that he could turn off the light switch and be in bed before the room got dark.
Buck O'Neil became the first African American coach in MLB history when he joined the Cubs coaching staff in 1962.
The reserve clause bound players to their teams indefinitely until Curt Flood challenged it in 1969, paving the way for free agency.
Free agency began in 1976 after an arbitrator ruled that pitchers Andy Messersmith and Dave McNally could become free agents, fundamentally changing the sport.
The first MLB draft was held in 1965. Rick Monday was the first overall pick, selected by the Kansas City Athletics.
Ken Griffey Sr. and Ken Griffey Jr. became the first father-son duo to play on the same MLB team simultaneously, with the 1990 Seattle Mariners.
On September 14, 1990, Ken Griffey Sr. and Ken Griffey Jr. hit back-to-back home runs for the Mariners, the only father-son combo to accomplish the feat.
The Atlanta Braves' 'Tomahawk Chop' chant became one of the most recognizable (and controversial) fan traditions in baseball.
The wave, a popular stadium crowd activity, is said to have originated at an Oakland A's game in 1981, though several other venues also claim its invention.
Bob Gibson's 1.12 ERA in 1968 prompted MLB to lower the pitcher's mound from 15 inches to 10 inches the following year to increase offense.
The 1968 season was called the 'Year of the Pitcher,' with Denny McLain winning 31 games for Detroit — the last pitcher to win 30 in a season.
In 1968, Carl Yastrzemski won the AL batting title with a .301 average, the lowest batting title-winning average in AL history.
Orel Hershiser set a record with 59 consecutive scoreless innings in 1988, breaking Don Drysdale's record of 58.2.
The 'Moneyball' revolution, led by Billy Beane and the 2002 Oakland A's, popularized the use of sabermetrics and on-base percentage as key offensive metrics.
Bill James, a night watchman at a pork and beans factory, pioneered sabermetrics in the 1970s and 1980s with his annual Baseball Abstract publications.
WAR (Wins Above Replacement) has become one of the most important advanced statistics in baseball, measuring a player's total contribution compared to a replacement-level player.
The 2002 Oakland A's won 20 consecutive games, an AL record, on their way to winning the division despite one of the lowest payrolls in baseball.
The 1906 World Series between the Cubs (who won 116 regular-season games) and the White Sox ('Hitless Wonders') is one of the biggest upsets in Series history.
The 2001 Seattle Mariners won 116 regular-season games, tying the 1906 Cubs for the most wins in a season, but were eliminated in the ALCS by the Yankees.
The designated hitter was first proposed by National League president John Heydler in 1928, but it wasn't adopted (by the AL only) until 1973.
David Ortiz was one of the most prolific designated hitters in history, hitting 541 career home runs and earning 10 All-Star selections, primarily as a DH.
Edgar Martinez, who spent his entire 18-year career with the Mariners, is considered the greatest DH ever and has the annual DH award named after him.
Frank Thomas, 'The Big Hurt,' is the only player in MLB history to have seven consecutive seasons with a .300+ average, 100+ RBIs, 100+ walks, and 20+ home runs.
Wade Boggs collected 3,010 career hits and won five AL batting titles. He famously ate chicken before every game as a superstitious ritual.
Tony Gwynn won eight NL batting titles, the most in National League history, and finished his career with a .338 batting average.
Tony Gwynn struck out only 434 times in 9,288 career plate appearances, an extraordinarily low rate for any era.
The sacrifice fly rule has been added and removed multiple times throughout baseball history. It was permanently restored in 1954.
The spitball was banned from baseball in 1920, but 17 pitchers who already used it were grandfathered in and allowed to continue throwing it.
Gaylord Perry was notorious for allegedly throwing illegal spitballs throughout his Hall of Fame career. His autobiography was titled 'Me and the Spitter.'
The 1989 World Series between the A's and Giants was interrupted by the Loma Prieta earthquake, delaying the Series for 10 days.
The longest nine-inning game in MLB history lasted 4 hours and 50 minutes between the Yankees and Red Sox on August 18, 2006.
Wilbert Robinson once tried to catch a grapefruit dropped from an airplane during spring training in 1915, thinking it was a baseball. It splattered on impact.
Eddie Gaedel, standing 3 feet 7 inches tall, was sent to bat for the St. Louis Browns in 1951 as a publicity stunt by owner Bill Veeck. He walked on four pitches.
Bill Veeck was one of baseball's greatest promoters, installing the first exploding scoreboard at Comiskey Park and once sending a pinch hitter on a motorcycle.
Disco Demolition Night at Comiskey Park on July 12, 1979, resulted in thousands of disco records being blown up on the field, causing a riot and a forfeit for the White Sox.
The 'Curse of the Billy Goat' began in 1945 when Billy Goat Tavern owner Billy Sianis was asked to leave Wrigley Field because his pet goat's odor was bothering fans.
The Cleveland Indians' 1948 World Series victory was their last until 2016, when they lost to the Cubs in seven games as the Guardians' predecessor.
The Philadelphia Phillies have the most losses of any professional sports franchise in North American history, surpassing 11,000 all-time losses.
The Phillies didn't win their first World Series until 1980, defeating the Royals, coming 97 years after the franchise was established in 1883.
The 2008 Phillies won the World Series in a game delayed by rain — the first time a World Series game was suspended and resumed.
The 2022 Phillies made the World Series after missing the playoffs for 11 consecutive years, but fell to the Houston Astros in six games.
The Houston Astros won legitimately in 2022, defeating the Phillies, somewhat redeeming the franchise after the 2017 sign-stealing scandal.
Alex Rodriguez signed a 10-year, $252 million contract with the Texas Rangers in 2000, the richest contract in baseball history at the time.
Alex Rodriguez was later traded to the Yankees and served a season-long suspension in 2014 for involvement with the Biogenesis PED clinic.
Giancarlo Stanton signed a 13-year, $325 million contract with the Marlins in 2014, which was the largest in MLB history at the time.
The Atlanta Braves' Greg Maddux, Tom Glavine, and John Smoltz formed one of the greatest pitching rotations in baseball history during the 1990s.
Tom Glavine won 305 career games and two Cy Young Awards, anchoring the Braves' rotation alongside Maddux and Smoltz.
John Smoltz is the only pitcher in MLB history to record both 200 wins and 150 saves in his career, showcasing his versatility as a starter and closer.
Jim Abbott, born without a right hand, pitched a no-hitter for the New York Yankees on September 4, 1993, against the Cleveland Indians.
Bo Jackson was a two-sport star who played both MLB and NFL. His prodigious power and speed made him one of the most electrifying athletes of the late 1980s.
Deion Sanders is the only athlete to play in both a World Series (1992 Braves) and a Super Bowl (1994 and 1995 49ers/Cowboys).
Michael Jordan played minor league baseball for the Birmingham Barons in 1994 during his first retirement from basketball, hitting .202.
The MLB draft is 20 rounds long as of 2022, reduced from 40 rounds. International amateur players are signed separately through a bonus pool system.
The Rule 5 draft allows teams to select players from other organizations who were not added to their 40-man roster, helping to prevent talent hoarding.
Tommy John surgery, named after the pitcher who first had it in 1974, reconstructs the ulnar collateral ligament in the elbow and has saved countless pitching careers.
Dr. Frank Jobe performed the first Tommy John surgery in 1974 and gave John only a 1-in-100 chance of returning. John came back to pitch 14 more seasons.
The earned run average (ERA) was first adopted as an official statistic by the American League in 1912 and the National League in 1917.
The batting helmet was not mandatory in MLB until 1971, though some players had voluntarily worn them since the 1940s after near-fatal beanings.
Ray Chapman of the Cleveland Indians is the only MLB player to die from being hit by a pitch, struck by Carl Mays on August 16, 1920.
Tony Conigliaro's promising career with the Red Sox was derailed when a pitch hit him in the face on August 18, 1967, severely damaging his eyesight.
The seventh-inning stretch tradition dates back to at least the 1860s, though its exact origins are debated. President William Howard Taft is sometimes credited.
The tradition of the U.S. President throwing out the first pitch on Opening Day began with William Howard Taft in 1910.
The first night game in MLB history was played on May 24, 1935, at Crosley Field in Cincinnati between the Reds and the Phillies.
The first MLB game televised was on August 26, 1939, a doubleheader between the Brooklyn Dodgers and Cincinnati Reds at Ebbets Field.
Vin Scully called Dodgers games for 67 years, from 1950 to 2016, making him the longest-tenured broadcaster with a single team in professional sports history.
Harry Caray's enthusiastic 'Holy Cow!' calls and his rendition of 'Take Me Out to the Ball Game' during the seventh-inning stretch made him a Cubs institution.
Ernie Harwell, the voice of the Detroit Tigers for 42 years, was traded from the Brooklyn Dodgers' broadcast booth for a catcher in 1948.
Bob Uecker, 'Mr. Baseball,' turned a mediocre six-year playing career (.200 average) into a legendary broadcasting career with the Milwaukee Brewers.
The 'Tools of Ignorance' is a tongue-in-cheek term for the catcher's equipment, coined because catching is the most physically demanding position.
A 'can of corn' is an easy fly ball to catch. The term likely comes from old-time grocery store clerks tipping cans off high shelves with a stick and catching them.
A 'Texas Leaguer' is a weakly hit ball that drops between the infielders and outfielders for a single, named after the Texas League minor league circuit.
The term 'bullpen' for the relief pitchers' area may come from Bull Durham tobacco signs that were common on outfield walls near where pitchers warmed up.
A 'Baltimore chop' is a batted ball that hits the ground near home plate and bounces high, allowing the batter to reach first base. It was perfected by the 1890s Orioles.
The 'hot corner' refers to third base because right-handed pull hitters hit hard line drives toward the position, giving the fielder little reaction time.
A 'walk-off' hit got its name from broadcaster Dennis Eckersley, describing how the losing team 'walks off' the field after the home team scores the winning run.
The 'Mendoza Line' — a .200 batting average — became a benchmark for offensive futility, named after light-hitting shortstop Mario Mendoza.
A 'Golden Sombrero' is when a batter strikes out four times in a single game. A 'Platinum Sombrero' is five strikeouts in one game.
The 'cycle' — hitting a single, double, triple, and home run in the same game — has been accomplished over 330 times in MLB history.
An immaculate inning, when a pitcher strikes out all three batters on exactly nine pitches, has occurred over 100 times in MLB history.
The unassisted triple play is one of the rarest events in baseball, having occurred only 15 times in MLB regular-season history.
The hidden ball trick, where a fielder conceals the ball to tag an unsuspecting runner, has been successfully executed dozens of times in MLB history, most recently in 2019.
Brooks Robinson is considered the greatest defensive third baseman in MLB history, winning 16 consecutive Gold Glove Awards from 1960 to 1975.
Ozzie Smith, 'The Wizard of Oz,' won 13 consecutive Gold Glove Awards at shortstop and was known for his acrobatic backflips during pregame introductions.
Roberto Alomar won 10 Gold Glove Awards at second base and is considered one of the most complete second basemen in baseball history.
Keith Hernandez won 11 consecutive Gold Glove Awards at first base from 1978 to 1988, revolutionizing defense at the position.
Willie Mays won 12 Gold Glove Awards in center field, combining elite defense with power hitting for one of the greatest all-around careers in history.
Andruw Jones is considered one of the best defensive center fielders ever, winning 10 consecutive Gold Glove Awards from 1998 to 2007.
The Triple Crown — leading the league in batting average, home runs, and RBIs — is one of baseball's rarest achievements. Miguel Cabrera last accomplished it in 2012.
Before Miguel Cabrera in 2012, the last Triple Crown winner was Carl Yastrzemski in 1967, a 45-year gap.
Only two pitchers have won the Triple Crown (leading in wins, ERA, and strikeouts) multiple times: Sandy Koufax (3 times) and Grover Cleveland Alexander (3 times).
Justin Verlander won the AL MVP and Cy Young Award in 2011 with the Tigers, going 24-5 with a 2.40 ERA and 250 strikeouts.
Max Scherzer has won three Cy Young Awards and once threw two no-hitters in a single season (2015) for the Washington Nationals.
Jacob deGrom's 2018 season for the Mets (1.70 ERA, 269 strikeouts) is one of the greatest pitching seasons ever, earning him the Cy Young despite a 10-9 record.
Gerrit Cole signed a nine-year, $324 million contract with the Yankees in 2019, the richest deal ever for a pitcher at that time.
The Houston Astros' sign-stealing scandal involved players banging on trash cans to signal what pitch was coming, as revealed in 2019.
Manager A.J. Hinch and GM Jeff Luhnow were suspended for a year and then fired by the Astros following MLB's investigation into the 2017 sign-stealing scheme.
Alex Cora, bench coach for the 2017 Astros, was also suspended for his role in the sign-stealing scandal before being rehired by the Red Sox in 2021.
The Chicago Cubs paid $2.7 million for their mascot Clark the Cub costume. The team also famously didn't have lights at Wrigley Field until 1988.
The San Diego Chicken, first appearing in 1974, is considered one of the first sports mascots and influenced mascot culture across all professional sports.
The Phillie Phanatic, introduced in 1978, is consistently voted as one of the best mascots in professional sports and has become a cultural icon in Philadelphia.
Blooper, the Atlanta Braves' mascot introduced in 2018, replaced the beloved Homer the Brave and initially received a mixed reception from fans.
Mr. Met, introduced in 1964, was the first live-action mascot in Major League Baseball history.
The 'rally cap' tradition — wearing a baseball cap inside out or backwards — is a superstitious ritual believed to inspire a comeback when a team is losing.
Manny Ramirez's quirky behavior earned the phrase 'Manny being Manny,' covering everything from disappearing inside the Green Monster to high-fiving fans during plays.
Mark 'The Bird' Fidrych talked to the baseball and groomed the mound with his hands, becoming one of baseball's most colorful characters during his brief career with the Tigers.
Bill 'Spaceman' Lee was known for his eccentric behavior, including claiming he sprinkled marijuana on his pancakes and throwing an eephus pitch in the World Series.
Yogi Berra was famous for his 'Yogi-isms' — paradoxical quotes like 'It ain't over 'til it's over' and 'Nobody goes there anymore. It's too crowded.'
Casey Stengel's rambling, double-talking 'Stengelese' was legendary, with the manager once telling Congress: 'I've been in baseball a hundred years.'
The 'eephus pitch' is an extremely slow, high-arcing pitch designed to disrupt a batter's timing. Rip Sewell popularized it in the 1940s.
A knuckleball pitcher grips the ball with fingertips or knuckles and throws with minimal spin, causing unpredictable movement. R.A. Dickey won the 2012 Cy Young throwing knuckleballs.
Tim Wakefield threw a knuckleball for the Red Sox for 17 seasons, finishing with 186 career wins, the most in Red Sox franchise history.
Hoyt Wilhelm was the first reliever inducted into the Hall of Fame. He threw a no-hitter in his first career start but spent most of his career in the bullpen.
Dennis Eckersley is unique in baseball history as both a starting pitcher who threw a no-hitter and a dominant closer who saved 390 games.
Rollie Fingers' handlebar mustache became one of the most recognizable looks in baseball history during the A's dynasty of the 1970s.
The 1979 Pittsburgh Pirates rallied behind their team song 'We Are Family' by Sister Sledge to win the World Series, coming back from a 3-1 deficit.
Dave Winfield accidentally killed a seagull with a thrown ball during a 1983 game in Toronto. He was briefly arrested for cruelty to animals afterward.
Jose Canseco had a fly ball bounce off his head and over the fence for a home run on May 26, 1993, one of the most embarrassing plays in MLB history.
In 2001, Randy Johnson won the World Series co-MVP with Curt Schilling, dominating the Yankees with three wins and a save in the seven-game series.
Pedro Martinez threw seven-plus innings of no-hit relief in a 1995 game for the Expos, one of the most dominant relief appearances in history.
The Curse of the Bambino, Billy Goat curse, and Cleveland's championship drought were all considered among baseball's most enduring curses before they were broken.
The 1991 World Series between the Twins and Braves is considered one of the greatest ever, with five games decided by one run and three going to extra innings.
Jack Morris pitched a 10-inning shutout in Game 7 of the 1991 World Series, leading the Minnesota Twins to a 1-0 victory over the Atlanta Braves.
Kirby Puckett's Game 6 catch and walk-off homer in the 1991 World Series are among the most memorable moments in postseason history.
The 2002 World Series saw the Anaheim Angels rally from a 3-2 series deficit and a 5-0 Game 6 deficit to defeat the Giants, partly fueled by the Rally Monkey.
Fred McGriff was known as the 'Crime Dog' and hit 493 career home runs. He was elected to the Hall of Fame by the Contemporary Baseball Era Committee in 2023.
Mike Piazza is considered the greatest hitting catcher in MLB history, with 427 home runs and a .308 career batting average. He was elected to the Hall of Fame in 2016.
Ivan Rodriguez, 'Pudge,' won 13 Gold Glove Awards at catcher, the most ever at the position, and was known for his cannon arm throwing out base stealers.
Johnny Bench revolutionized catching by pioneering the one-handed catching technique and won 10 Gold Glove Awards with the Reds.
Yogi Berra won 10 World Series championships as a player with the Yankees, more than any other player in MLB history.
Whitey Ford holds the record for the most World Series wins by a pitcher with 10, all with the New York Yankees.
The designated hitter was first used in an MLB regular-season game on April 6, 1973, when Ron Blomberg of the Yankees batted as the DH and walked.
Instant replay was first introduced to MLB on a limited basis in 2008 for home run calls only. It was expanded to cover most calls starting in 2014.
The MLB challenge system, implemented in 2014, allows managers to challenge certain calls using instant replay, with reviews conducted at a centralized replay center in New York.
Robo-umps (Automated Ball-Strike system) were tested in the minor leagues starting in 2019 and have been gradually implemented, with plans for expanded MLB use.
The 'neighborhood play' at second base, where a fielder didn't need to touch the bag on a double play, was eliminated by rule change in 2016.
Chase Utley's hard slide into Ruben Tejada during the 2015 NLDS led to stricter sliding rules at second base, commonly called the 'Utley Rule.'
Home plate collision rules were implemented in 2014 (the 'Buster Posey Rule') after Posey's devastating leg injury on a play at the plate in 2011.
The 'ghost runner' rule, placing an automatic runner on second base in extra innings, was introduced during the 2020 COVID season and made permanent in 2023.
The 'mercy rule' in baseball is used in some amateur leagues but does not exist in MLB. The 2023 World Baseball Classic used a mercy rule of a 10-run lead after 7 innings.
The World Baseball Classic, first held in 2006, is an international tournament featuring national teams. Japan won the first two events and the 2023 edition.
Shohei Ohtani struck out Mike Trout, his Angels teammate, to end the 2023 World Baseball Classic final, giving Japan the championship.
The Olympic Games featured baseball from 1992 to 2008, then dropped it before bringing it back for the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, where Japan won gold.
Cuba dominated international baseball for decades, winning 25 of 28 Baseball World Cup tournaments from 1939 to 2011.
MLB's Opening Day is treated as an unofficial holiday in Cincinnati, where the Reds traditionally host the first game of the season due to their status as baseball's oldest professional team.
The Rawlings Gold Glove Award has been given annually since 1957 to the best fielder at each position in both the American and National Leagues.
The Roberto Clemente Award, given annually since 1971, recognizes the MLB player who best exemplifies sportsmanship, community involvement, and contribution to their team.
The Hank Aaron Award, given since 1999, honors the best overall offensive performer in each league, as determined by a combination of fan votes and a panel.
Cap Anson, one of the first great MLB hitters in the 1880s, was also a key figure in establishing the color barrier that kept Black players out of organized baseball until 1947.
The 1869 Cincinnati Red Stockings were the first openly professional baseball team, going undefeated in their inaugural season with a record of 57-0.
The National League was founded in 1876, and the American League was established as a major league in 1901, ending the NL's monopoly on professional baseball.
Abner Doubleday was long credited with inventing baseball in Cooperstown in 1839, but historians have debunked this myth. The sport evolved from earlier bat-and-ball games.
Alexander Cartwright is now more widely credited as the father of modern baseball, establishing rules like the diamond shape, foul lines, and three-strike outs in 1845.
The first enclosed baseball park was Cammeyer's Union Grounds in Brooklyn, which opened in 1862 and charged admission.
Forbes Field in Pittsburgh, opened in 1909, was the first ballpark made of concrete and steel rather than wood.
Ebbets Field, home of the Brooklyn Dodgers, was demolished in 1960 and replaced with an apartment complex, breaking the hearts of Brooklyn fans.
The Polo Grounds, home to the New York Giants, had an unusual bathtub shape with extremely short foul lines (279 feet in left, 258 in right) and a cavernous center field (483 feet).
Tropicana Field, home of the Tampa Bay Rays, is the last fixed domed stadium in MLB and has been criticized for its catwalk interference with fly balls.
Oracle Park (formerly AT&T Park) in San Francisco features 'McCovey Cove,' where kayakers collect home run balls that splash into the bay beyond right field.
PNC Park in Pittsburgh is widely considered one of the most beautiful ballparks in MLB, offering stunning views of the Allegheny River and the downtown skyline.
The 'Green Monster seats' atop Fenway Park's left field wall were added in 2003, creating some of the most unique and coveted seats in all of sports.
Target Field in Minneapolis was the first open-air stadium built in a cold-weather climate since the 1990s when it opened in 2010, replacing the Metrodome.
Globe Life Field in Arlington, Texas, features a retractable roof and hosted the entire 2020 postseason, including the World Series, due to COVID-19.
Coors Field in Denver is known as a hitter's paradise because the thin mile-high air allows baseballs to travel farther, inflating offensive statistics.
The Rockies store their baseballs in a humidor at Coors Field to reduce the effect of altitude on the ball. This practice was adopted in 2002.
Chase Field in Phoenix was the first MLB stadium built with a retractable roof and natural grass, opening in 1998.
George Steinbrenner owned the Yankees from 1973 until his death in 2010, becoming one of the most notorious and successful owners in sports history.
Charlie Finley, owner of the Oakland A's, introduced colored uniforms, white shoes, and a mascot mule named Charlie O. in the 1970s, flouting baseball tradition.
Bill Veeck, owner of several teams, is the only owner in the Baseball Hall of Fame. He sent a midget to bat, created the exploding scoreboard, and integrated the AL.
Branch Rickey, as GM of the Brooklyn Dodgers, signed Jackie Robinson and broke baseball's color barrier. He also invented the minor league farm system while with the Cardinals.
The farm system, pioneered by Branch Rickey in the 1920s, allows MLB teams to develop young players through affiliated minor league teams at various skill levels.
The Houston Astros went from an expansion team in 1962 to arguably the most dominant franchise in the early 2020s, winning the World Series in 2017 and 2022.
The Miami Marlins have never won a division title but have won two World Series (1997, 2003), both times as a wild card team.
The Milwaukee Brewers are the only franchise to have played in both the American League (1969-1997) and the National League (1998-present).
The Seattle Mariners hold the distinction of being one of the few MLB franchises to have never appeared in a World Series.
The Colorado Rockies made the World Series in 2007 after winning 21 of their last 22 games, but were swept by the Boston Red Sox.
The Arizona Diamondbacks won the World Series in only their fourth year of existence in 2001, the fastest expansion team to win a championship.
Jim Thome hit 612 career home runs, one of the most prolific power hitters in history, but rarely received the attention of contemporaries like Bonds and Sosa.
Sammy Sosa hit 60+ home runs three times (1998, 1999, 2001) but never led the league in any of those seasons.
Mark McGwire hit 583 career home runs and admitted to using performance-enhancing drugs, preventing his election to the Hall of Fame through the traditional ballot.
Jeff Bagwell and Craig Biggio both spent their entire careers with the Houston Astros and were called the 'Killer B's.' Both are Hall of Famers.
Craig Biggio was hit by a pitch 285 times in his career, the most in the modern era, and his uniform was perpetually dirty from crowding the plate.
Cal Ripken Jr. voluntarily ended his consecutive games streak at 2,632 on September 20, 1998, by taking himself out of the lineup.
Roberto Clemente had exactly 3,000 hits when he died in a plane crash. Had he survived, he surely would have added to that total significantly.
Lou Gehrig's ALS diagnosis in 1939 ended his consecutive games streak at 2,130. ALS is now commonly known as 'Lou Gehrig's Disease.'
Ted Williams missed nearly five full seasons of his career due to military service in World War II and the Korean War. He still hit 521 home runs.
Ted Williams once said that hitting a baseball is the single most difficult thing to do in sports, noting that failing 7 out of 10 times is considered excellent.
Joe DiMaggio was named the 'Greatest Living Player' in a 1969 poll and insisted on being introduced that way at public events for the rest of his life.
Mickey Mantle played his entire career with chronic knee injuries dating back to his teenage years, making his accomplishments even more remarkable.
Willie Mays hit 660 career home runs and made 'The Catch' that many consider the greatest play in baseball history.
Hank Aaron overcame racism and death threats to become one of the most respected figures in baseball history, finishing with 755 home runs and 2,297 RBIs.
Stan Musial spent his entire 22-year career with the St. Louis Cardinals, earning three MVP awards and hitting .331 with 475 home runs.
Honus Wagner won eight NL batting titles and is considered the greatest shortstop of the dead-ball era, playing primarily for the Pittsburgh Pirates.
Christy Mathewson won 373 games and threw three shutouts in the 1905 World Series for the New York Giants.
Walter Johnson's fastball was nicknamed 'The Big Train.' He won the AL MVP twice and had a career ERA of 2.17.
Lefty Grove won 300 games despite not reaching the majors until age 25. His nine ERA titles are the most in MLB history.
Bob Feller enlisted in the Navy the day after Pearl Harbor, missing nearly four prime seasons. He still won 266 games and threw three no-hitters.
Warren Spahn won 363 games, the most by any left-handed pitcher in history, and didn't win his first game until age 25 due to military service.
Whitey Ford's career winning percentage of .690 is the highest among 20th-century pitchers with at least 150 wins.
Steve Carlton won four Cy Young Awards, including the 1972 award when he went 27-10 for a Phillies team that won only 59 games — he accounted for 46% of their wins.
Bob Gibson's 1.12 ERA in 1968 is the lowest single-season ERA in the live-ball era (post-1920). He struck out 17 batters in Game 1 of that year's World Series.
Tom Glavine was drafted by the NHL's Los Angeles Kings as a hockey player in the same year (1984) he was drafted by the Braves.
Catfish Hunter became the first big-money free agent when he signed a five-year, $3.75 million deal with the Yankees in 1974, monumental at the time.
Jim 'Catfish' Hunter got his nickname from A's owner Charlie Finley, who thought it would make a better story. Hunter actually had no connection to catfishing.
Nolan Ryan once charged the mound after being hit by a pitch and put Robin Ventura in a headlock, despite being 46 years old at the time.
Nolan Ryan had a career ERA of 3.19 — good but not elite — yet his 5,714 strikeouts and seven no-hitters make him one of baseball's most legendary pitchers.
Pete Rose played 24 MLB seasons and was named to 17 All-Star teams at five different positions: first base, second base, third base, left field, and right field.
A baseball weighs between 5 and 5.25 ounces and has a circumference of 9 to 9.25 inches. Each ball starts as a small cork center wrapped tightly in yarn.
MLB umpires are required to wear black underwear in case they rip their pants during a game.
The 'K' symbol for a strikeout was invented by journalist Henry Chadwick in the 1860s because 'K' was the last letter in 'struck,' signifying 'struck out.'
A backwards 'K' (Ꝁ) in a scorebook indicates a called third strike, where the batter didn't swing.
The earliest baseball games were played without gloves, masks, or protective gear. Catchers sometimes stood several feet behind the plate.
The catcher's mask was invented in 1877 by Fred Thayer, a Harvard player, and was initially ridiculed as unmanly.
The first batting gloves were used by Bobby Thomson in the 1940s, though they didn't become widely popular until Ken Harrelson made them fashionable in the 1960s.
Pine tar is legal on bats up to 18 inches from the knob but illegal on pitches. Several pitchers have been ejected for using pine tar or similar sticky substances.
In 2021, MLB began cracking down on pitchers using sticky substances, leading to mid-game checks and several high-profile ejections.
Corked bats, which are hollowed out and filled with cork to make them lighter, are illegal. Sammy Sosa was caught using one in 2003.
The sacrifice bunt has declined significantly in modern baseball due to analytics showing that giving up an out rarely improves run-scoring probability.
Launch angle and exit velocity became key hitting metrics in the 2010s, contributing to the 'fly ball revolution' that prioritized home runs over contact hitting.
The shift — overloading one side of the field with defenders — became so prevalent in the 2010s and 2020s that MLB banned extreme shifts starting in 2023.
The closer role as we know it today wasn't common until the 1980s. Before that, relief pitchers often pitched multiple innings in various situations.
The opener strategy, where a relief pitcher starts the game for an inning or two before a 'bulk pitcher' takes over, was popularized by the Tampa Bay Rays in 2018.
The four-man pitching rotation was standard until the 1970s, when teams gradually shifted to a five-man rotation to reduce wear on pitchers' arms.
In 2023, MLB introduced bigger bases (15 inches square instead of 15x15 inches, up from the previous standard), partly to reduce injuries and encourage stolen bases.
The intentional walk was changed in 2017 to allow managers to simply point the batter to first base without throwing four pitches.
Jackie Robinson's jersey number 42 was universally retired by MLB in 1997. Every player in MLB wears 42 on April 15 each year in his honor.
The first interleague play in MLB history occurred in 1997, ending the tradition of AL and NL teams only meeting in the World Series and All-Star Game.
Wild card playoff berths were introduced in 1995. The format has expanded several times, including in 2012 (wild card game) and 2022 (three wild card teams per league).
The 2022 expanded playoff format added a third wild card team in each league and introduced a best-of-three wild card round, increasing the playoff field to 12 teams.
The dead-ball era (roughly 1900-1919) was characterized by low-scoring games, dominant pitching, and baseballs that were used until they fell apart.
The live-ball era began around 1920 when cleaner balls were mandated after Ray Chapman's death and Babe Ruth's explosive power changed the game's strategy.
The Black Sox scandal of 1919 led to the creation of the Commissioner of Baseball position, with Judge Kenesaw Mountain Landis appointed as the first commissioner.
Commissioner Kenesaw Mountain Landis banned the eight Black Sox players from baseball for life, including 'Shoeless' Joe Jackson, despite Jackson's strong performance in the Series.
The 'Midsummer Classic' (All-Star Game) was originally proposed by Chicago Tribune sports editor Arch Ward as part of the 1933 Chicago World's Fair.
Babe Ruth hit the first All-Star Game home run in 1933 at Comiskey Park, leading the American League to a 4-2 victory.
The most lopsided game in modern MLB history was a 30-3 Rangers victory over the Orioles on August 22, 2007.
In 2007, the Colorado Rockies won 21 of their last 22 games to reach the World Series, capped by a dramatic 163rd-game tiebreaker win, before being swept by the Red Sox.
The 'infield fly rule' was first adopted in 1895 to prevent fielders from deliberately dropping pop-ups to get easy force outs on base runners.
Joe Niekro was caught with an emery board and sandpaper in his pocket while pitching in 1987, leading to a 10-game suspension for doctoring the baseball.

The pine tar on George Brett's bat in the 1983 Pine Tar Game extended 24 inches up the handle — 6 inches beyond the allowed limit — but the rule only stipulates removal of the bat, not nullification of the play.
A 'rain delay' can last indefinitely. The longest rain delay in MLB history was 7 hours and 23 minutes during a 1990 White Sox game.
No-hitters where the pitcher or team still loses are possible if runs score via walks, errors, and hit batsmen. It has happened multiple times in MLB history.
The 'ground rule double' occurs when a fair ball bounces over the outfield fence. The batter is awarded second base, and any runners advance two bases.
The 'pine tar incident' of 1983 was resolved when AL President Lee MacPhail overruled the umpires, reinstated Brett's home run, and the final four outs were played 25 days later.
Dock Ellis wore hair curlers on the mound, which became one of the defining images of 1970s baseball counterculture.
Ted Williams famously refused to tip his cap to fans, maintaining his stubborn independence throughout his career with the Boston Red Sox.
Ty Cobb was known for his aggressive style of play, including sharpening his spikes to intimidate fielders, and his .366 career average remains the highest ever.
Rogers Hornsby hit .424 in 1924, the highest single-season batting average in the modern era (post-1900).
Nap Lajoie hit .426 in 1901, the highest AL batting average on record, though some historians dispute whether it was achieved under modern conditions.
The most home runs hit in a single MLB game by one team is 10, accomplished by the Toronto Blue Jays against the Baltimore Orioles on September 14, 1987.
Fernando Tatis Sr. hit two grand slams in one inning on April 23, 1999, driving in eight runs in the third inning alone — an all-time record.
Mark Whiten hit four home runs and drove in 12 runs in a single game on September 7, 1993, tying MLB records for both stats in one game.
Shawn Green hit four home runs in a single game on May 23, 2002, accumulating 19 total bases — an MLB record that still stands.
Harvey Haddix pitched 12 perfect innings on May 26, 1959, for the Pirates against the Braves but lost the game 1-0 in the 13th inning.
The 1903 World Series was a best-of-nine format. It wasn't until 1905 that the best-of-seven format became standard.
Commissioner Bud Selig served from 1998 to 2015 and oversaw revenue growth, expanded playoffs, and drug testing, but also the 1994 strike and the steroid era.
Rob Manfred became MLB Commissioner in 2015 and has pushed for faster games, rule changes, and expanded international play.
The 2023 rule changes (pitch clock, shift ban, bigger bases) were credited with making MLB games more exciting and faster, leading to increased attendance.
The 'hot dog cannon' — a T-shirt cannon modified to shoot hot dogs — is a fan favorite at many minor league parks across America.
Minor league baseball promotions include Thirsty Thursday, bark-in-the-park nights, and themes like Star Wars Night and Seinfeld Night, drawing fans for entertainment beyond the game.
The Durham Bulls, made famous by the movie Bull Durham, are one of the most well-known minor league teams, playing in the Triple-A level.
The Savannah Bananas, a Coastal Plain League team, became a viral sensation with their exhibition tours featuring choreographed dances, trick plays, and 'Banana Ball' rules.
The longest professional baseball game was a 33-inning contest between the Pawtucket Red Sox and Rochester Red Wings in 1981, lasting 8 hours and 25 minutes over two days.
Wade Boggs allegedly drank 64 beers on a cross-country flight, a claim that became one of baseball's most legendary anecdotes.
'The Natural' (1984) starring Robert Redford is based on Bernard Malamud's novel, partly inspired by the real-life shooting of Eddie Waitkus by a stalker in 1949.
The 'Sandlot' (1993) is one of the most beloved baseball movies, giving the world the iconic line 'You're killing me, Smalls!'
'Eight Men Out' (1988) dramatized the 1919 Black Sox scandal, based on Eliot Asinof's nonfiction book of the same name.
'42' (2013) starred Chadwick Boseman as Jackie Robinson and depicted Robinson's struggle to break baseball's color barrier in 1947.
The Designated Hitter was first used in a World Series game in 1976, and was used by the AL team only until the universal DH was adopted in 2022.
Mike Schmidt is widely regarded as the greatest third baseman in MLB history, winning three MVP awards and 10 Gold Gloves for the Philadelphia Phillies.
George Brett finished with a .305 career average, 3,154 hits, and 317 home runs, making him one of the most complete hitters in history.
Ryne Sandberg won the NL MVP in 1984 with the Cubs and was known for his outstanding defense at second base, winning nine Gold Gloves.
Dave Winfield was drafted by four professional sports teams in three different sports (MLB, NBA, NFL, and ABA) upon graduating from the University of Minnesota in 1973.
Andre Dawson, 'The Hawk,' won the 1987 NL MVP while playing for the last-place Chicago Cubs, one of the few MVPs from a losing team.
Kirby Puckett's career was cut short by glaucoma in 1996 at age 35. He had 2,304 hits, six All-Star selections, and two World Series rings.
Don Mattingly, 'Donnie Baseball,' was the face of the Yankees in the 1980s but never reached the World Series as a player despite exceptional talent.
The 'Bash Brothers,' Jose Canseco and Mark McGwire, combined for 618 home runs during their time together on the Oakland A's from 1986 to 1992.
Dwight Gooden, 'Doc,' won the Cy Young Award in 1985 at age 20 with a 24-4 record and 1.53 ERA — one of the most dazzling pitching seasons ever.
Darryl Strawberry hit 335 career home runs but his career was derailed by substance abuse issues that kept him from reaching his immense potential.
The 1986 Mets are considered one of the most talented and wild teams in history, winning the World Series while being known for their partying lifestyle.
Ozzie Smith's bare-handed grab against the Expos and his acrobatic style earned him the reputation as the most entertaining defensive player in baseball history.
The 2015 Kansas City Royals were known for their remarkable ability to come back late in games, earning the nickname 'Comeback Kids' en route to the championship.
Madison Bumgarner's 2014 postseason performance is considered one of the greatest in history — he went 4-1 with a 1.03 ERA, including a five-inning save in Game 7.
Buster Posey won three World Series rings, an MVP, and a batting title with the Giants before retiring in 2021, becoming a modern Giants legend.
Bryce Harper won MVP awards in both leagues (NL with the Nationals in 2015 and Phillies in 2021) and hit a dramatic NLCS walk-off homer in 2022.
Mookie Betts won the AL MVP and World Series with the Red Sox in 2018, then won another World Series with the Dodgers in 2020 after a blockbuster trade.
Juan Soto was traded from the Nationals to the Padres in 2022 in one of the biggest midseason trades in recent memory, despite being only 23 years old.
Ronald Acuña Jr. became the first player in MLB history to hit 40 home runs and steal 70 bases in a single season in 2023, winning the NL MVP unanimously.
Freddie Freeman won the 2020 NL MVP with the Braves, then signed with the Dodgers and won his second World Series ring in 2020 and continued to be one of MLB's best hitters.
José Altuve stands 5 feet 6 inches tall, making him one of the shortest players in modern MLB history. He won three batting titles and the 2017 AL MVP.
Corey Seager won World Series MVP in 2020 with the Dodgers and again in 2023 with the Rangers, becoming one of the most accomplished postseason performers of his era.
Yordan Alvarez hit a dramatic three-run walk-off homer in Game 6 of the 2022 ALCS to send the Astros to the World Series.
The shift ban implemented in 2023 helped left-handed hitters significantly, as extreme shifts had previously taken away many hits up the middle.
The 2023 season saw a significant increase in stolen bases due to the pitch clock, bigger bases, and limits on pickoff attempts.
Trea Turner hit 15 RBIs in the 2022 NLCS for the Phillies and became one of the most exciting players in baseball with his elite speed and bat.
The Mendoza Line (.200 batting average) was popularized by George Brett and other players who jokingly referenced shortstop Mario Mendoza's struggles at the plate.
A 'quality start' is defined as a start in which the pitcher lasts at least six innings and allows no more than three earned runs, introduced as a stat in 1985.
The 'save' statistic was not an official MLB stat until 1969, despite closers having fulfilled a similar role for decades before that.
WHIP (Walks and Hits per Inning Pitched) was invented by fantasy baseball writer Daniel Okrent in 1979 and has since become a standard baseball statistic.
Fantasy baseball was invented in 1980 by Daniel Okrent and a group of friends who formed the 'Rotisserie League' at La Rotisserie Française restaurant in New York.
The first baseball video game was 'Baseball' for the Magnavox Odyssey in 1972, one of the earliest video games of any kind.
R.B.I. Baseball, first released in 1987, was one of the first baseball video games to use real player names and stats from the MLBPA license.
MLB The Show is the current premier baseball video game franchise, originally exclusive to PlayStation before becoming available on Xbox and Nintendo Switch.
'Bases loaded' means runners are on first, second, and third base simultaneously. A home run in this situation is called a 'grand slam,' worth four runs.
The most grand slams in a career were hit by Alex Rodriguez with 25, surpassing Lou Gehrig's long-standing record of 23.
Travis Hafner held the record for most grand slams in a single season with 6 in 2006, tying Don Mattingly's 1987 record.
The 'suicide squeeze' is one of the most exciting plays in baseball — the runner on third breaks for home before the pitch, and the batter must bunt the ball.
A 'switch hitter' bats from both sides of the plate. Mickey Mantle is the most famous switch hitter in history, and Chipper Jones was the best of the modern era.
Chipper Jones, the switch-hitting third baseman for the Braves, won the 1999 NL MVP and retired with a .303 career average, named his son Shea after hitting so well at Shea Stadium.
The 'Tommy John epidemic' has led to concerns about pitcher workloads, with hundreds of pitchers undergoing the surgery since it was first performed in 1974.
The 'dead ball' used in early baseball was softer and less tightly wound, contributing to low-scoring games. The transition to a 'live ball' around 1920 boosted offense dramatically.
George Herman 'Babe' Ruth started his career as an elite left-handed pitcher for the Red Sox before becoming the greatest slugger of his era with the Yankees.
Babe Ruth's 1927 record of 60 home runs was hit in a season with only 154 games, making it arguably more impressive per-game than later records.
Babe Ruth was an orphan and grew up in a Baltimore reform school, where he learned to play baseball. He was discovered and signed at age 19.
The term 'southpaw' for left-handed pitchers may originate from early ballparks being oriented so that a left-hander's throwing arm was on the south side of the diamond.
A pitcher who throws a 'complete game shutout' must pitch the entire game without allowing any runs — a feat that has become increasingly rare in modern baseball.
In 2023, Spencer Strider of the Braves struck out 281 batters in just 186.2 innings, showcasing the modern trend of high-strikeout, high-velocity pitching.
Aroldis Chapman threw the fastest recorded pitch in MLB history at 105.1 mph on September 24, 2010, while pitching for the Cincinnati Reds.
The average MLB fastball velocity has increased from about 89 mph in 2002 to over 93 mph by the 2020s, contributing to record-high strikeout rates.
The splitter, or split-finger fastball, was popularized by Bruce Sutter in the late 1970s and Roger Craig taught it to many pitchers in the 1980s.
The slider has become the most popular secondary pitch in modern baseball, thrown by over 70% of pitchers, valued for its late-breaking movement.
The changeup is effective because it mimics the arm action of a fastball but arrives 8-12 mph slower, disrupting the batter's timing.
The cutter, a variation of the fastball that breaks slightly, was Mariano Rivera's signature pitch and is now one of the most popular pitches in baseball.
The curveball was first thrown by Candy Cummings in the 1870s, initially dismissed as an optical illusion before science confirmed that a spinning ball does indeed curve.
MLB currently has 30 teams divided into two leagues (AL and NL) of three divisions each (East, Central, West), with five teams per division.
The New York Yankees' pinstripes were introduced in 1912 and have become the most iconic uniform design in baseball history.
The Chicago White Sox wore shorts as part of their uniform for three games in 1976, one of the most unusual uniform experiments in baseball history.
The Pittsburgh Pirates' pillbox caps of the 1970s were controversial but have become a nostalgic fan favorite in modern throwback uniform promotions.
The Houston Astros' 'tequila sunrise' rainbow uniforms of the 1970s and 1980s are either beloved or mocked, depending on who you ask, but remain iconic.
The 2023 City Connect uniform program introduced alternate jerseys that celebrate the local culture and identity of each team's city, becoming a major merchandising success.
Batting practice (BP) before games has become less common in recent years, with many teams reducing or eliminating the pregame fan attraction.
Walk-up songs, where players choose personal music to play as they approach the plate, became popular in the 1990s and are now a beloved part of game presentation.
Closer entrance songs, like Mariano Rivera's 'Enter Sandman' by Metallica, have become iconic parts of baseball culture and stadium atmospherics.
Trevor Hoffman entered to AC/DC's 'Hells Bells,' creating one of the most intimidating closer entrances in baseball history.
The ceremonial first pitch has been thrown by presidents, celebrities, and fans for over a century, though some memorable ones have gone hilariously off-target.
50 Cent's wildly off-target ceremonial first pitch at a Mets game in 2014 is considered one of the worst first pitches ever thrown.
Hot dogs are the most popular food at MLB stadiums, with fans consuming an estimated 18.5 million hot dogs per season across all 30 parks.
Dodger Stadium is famous for its Dodger Dogs, a 10-inch pork frank that has been a fan favorite since the stadium opened in 1962.
The seventh-inning stretch has become associated with singing 'Take Me Out to the Ball Game,' especially at Wrigley Field where celebrity guest conductors lead the crowd.
'God Bless America' has been performed at many MLB games during the seventh-inning stretch since the September 11, 2001, attacks, particularly at Yankee Stadium.
The commissioner's trophy, awarded to the World Series champion, is the only major North American championship trophy that is newly made each year rather than passed on.
